article_meta.title: A California judge scolded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team after they showed up to a Los Angeles courtroom weari

reasoning: The model correctly identifies author-prose language and avoids flagging quoted text. All three flagged items are from the author's own prose. The identified items are reasonable but somewhat debatable - 'high-stakes' and 'closely watched' are common journalistic descriptors that could be seen as mild bias or standard language. 'Big Tech' is a reasonable flag as loaded word choice. The model missed 'strike at the heart of the national debate over Big Tech's influence on America's youth' which is a more clearly sensationalized/editorialized framing by the author. The model also could have flagged 'scolded' in the headline/lede as a loaded verb (Spin) - the author chose 'scolded' over more neutral alternatives like 'admonished' or 'warned.' Overall, attribution rule compliance is perfect, but there are some missed instances and some questionable flags.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: All three flagged items are from the author's own prose. No quoted text was flagged. The model correctly excluded Ward's quote, the judge's statements, and other attributed language.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: 'High-stakes trial' and 'closely watched trial' are somewhat standard journalistic phrases, though they do carry evaluative weight. 'Big Tech' is a defensible flag as loaded word choice. These are in a gray zone - defensible but not the strongest examples.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed 'scolded' in the opening (a loaded verb choice by the author - 'Spin'), and 'strike at the heart of the national debate over Big Tech's influence on America's youth' which is clearly editorialized/sensationalized framing in the author's own prose. These are arguably more significant bias instances than some that were flagged.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: The labels are reasonable. 'High-stakes' as Sensationalism is defensible. 'Closely watched' as Subjective Qualifying Adjective works. 'Big Tech' as Word Choice is correct. No major mislabeling.
